title: Why Law Firms Are Shrinking Their Footprints
verdict: Professional services tenants are trading volume for signal, taking less space but in better buildings.
evidence: Major law firms renewed or relocated into an average of 30% less square footage between 2023 and 2025, according to brokerage data across the top 20 U.S. office markets. But the space they are taking is dramatically more expensive per foot, concentrated in trophy and near-trophy buildings with premium amenities, prominent addresses, and recent capital improvements. Firms report that the shift reflects both hybrid work realities and a conscious decision to use office space as a recruiting and client-facing tool rather than a warehouse for associates. Several Am Law 100 firms have explicitly cited building quality and lobby experience as deciding factors in relocation decisions.
frame: The professional services flight to quality is now a structural trend, not a cyclical adjustment. For office building owners, this creates a widening gap between the buildings that attract these tenants and the ones that lose them. The winners are properties that invest in arrival experience, shared amenity floors, and the kind of architectural distinction that a managing partner can point to when justifying the lease to the partnership. The losers are commodity Class A buildings that competed on price and parking ratios. In this market, the building itself is part of the tenant's brand, and landlords who understand that will set rents accordingly.
